sentences.text: Yeah, in our transport there were about 55, maybe 60 kids under 16.
There wasn't anybody really younger than 12 or maybe 11, because the little kids-- they had all been taken away during that whole process.
Some of them were taken away to the-- well, some Gentiles hid some of them, but there weren't any little kids in our transport.
So we got there.
And I remember it was raining.
It was a very rainy day.
And we didn't know where the heck we were marching.
And we got to camp.
And the camp commandant addressed us.
And he was not really a very high ranking officer.
It was only a 3,000 people camp.
I think all he was a sergeant.
Do you remember his name?
No, I don't.
And he was an oberscharfuhrer, sergeant.
And he said-- the reason I remember what he said is because my father was so impressed by what he said because quite unlike everybody else, he addressed us as meine herren, gentlemen.
And Germans don't usually address Jews as meine herren.
And of course, he was being sarcastic.
But it made a point.
I mean, usually they address us as Judischer schweinehund.
But so he said, meine herren, he said, welcome to Dachau, he said.
I'm sure that all of you-- most of you have heard of Dachau, but most of you don't believe the things you have heard.
Well, let me disillusion you.
Everything you've heard is absolutely true, he says.
And furthermore, some of the things that happened here, you never heard about.
However, that's unimportant.
He said, what is important is that you should understand one thing.
You're all dead.
Not a single one of you will ever leave this camp alive.
As long as you work, nobody will bother you.
However, you're not leaving, even if Germany were to lose the war, which is unthinkable at this point.
Five minutes before midnight, we will get rid of you.
So just keep that thought in mind.
And we'll all get along very nicely.
And now, we'll do calisthenics for a traditional Dachau welcome.
And then they came, and, you know, we had to run and drop.
And they chased us, and they hit us with a whip.
So when we're totally filthy, they took us to take showers.
So they shaved our heads, and they took our clothes away and went through the showers.
And then they issued us the striped uniforms.
And we got a pair of pants and a jacket and a shirt and underpants.
That's all there was.
And then two pieces of fabric to wrap around your feet, and shoes, which was wooden soles and canvas tops type of things.
And everybody got a number.
And my number was 82191.
It was sewed onto my striped beret and my jacket and on my pants.
My father was 82192.
And they didn't talk to us.
My mother wasn't there.
My mother was taken away at Stutthof.
This is when we got off of the transport where all the women were taken.
There were no women-- none.
We got some women in the camp much later, which was kind of unusual-- Hungarian women with their heads already shaved.
But there were no women.
So we were-- then we were-- we went to the barracks where we were going to sleep.
And the arrangement was it was like a trench in the ground.
And above the ground, there stood a triangular roof, and it was a long thing, about-- I don't know, 20, 30 yards along the roof.
And the trench, you walked down into the trench with little steps.
And on both-- on the shoulders of the trench, right under the roof, there were boards.
And on the boards, there was straw and blankets.
And it was like 35 people lying next to each other one side and 35 people lying on the other side.
And this is where-- how you slept.
On one side was a door.
On the other side was a little window and a little stove.
The stove was where we would sit around in the evening and pick out the lice and throw them into the fire because if one person had lice, everybody had lice.
And like in all camps, there was no way to wash anything or launder anything or even wash yourself.
So pretty soon everybody was just covered.
And it killed a lot of people because it itches and you're filthy and you scratch and gets infected and you die.
Well, this is what it is.
There were no children officially.
And this is one of the weird things the way the German mind works.
Shortly after we got there, we were told that this is going to be a labor camp that's going to be too hard for children, and all the children should register.
And they'll be taken away to be shown how to learn a craft-- you know, locksmithing or, I don't know, carpentry-- for the war effort.
And it sounded perfectly reasonable.
So most kids went when the transport came to take them away.
Me and maybe five or six others didn't.
I didn't want to go.
And they were searching through the barracks.
I've hid-- I hid in latrine.
They didn't find me.
And then the kids were taken away.
After the war, I found out that they were taken to where Mengele was performing experiments and that most of them died.
Some of them survived.

As a matter of fact, to my amazement, I just read an article by somebody, a young man who appeared-- a kid who was part of the group and did manage to survive and apparently was still in his right mind.
I didn't know there were any.
But I stayed behind.
But the thing is, you see, that even though I was only 13, there weren't supposed to be any kids left.
All the kids were taken away.
It was the commandant's responsibility that there were no kids.
So obviously, if they found a 13-year-old kid, it would be a black mark against him.
So naturally, officially I became 18.
And everybody you know-- of course, the more elegant solution, which obviously didn't occur to him, was to shoot me.
But he didn't think of it.
[NON-ENGLISH].
So instead he made me 18, and I had the same rights and privileges as everybody else, and me and the other four or five kids.
The work we did-- at first, I was very, very lucky because some of the Jews within the camp, you know, who were in positions of authority from Lithuania, they knew me.
They knew my father.
I got a job inside the camp.
And my job at first-- my first job, was cleaning latrines, which sounds disgusting, but actually it was very good because these boards with big holes in them and all that.
And naturally, there was no facilities and no toilet paper and people had diarrhea.
And winter especially, you'd get piles that would be frozen crap all over the place.
And we'd get in in the morning and start cleaning them up, me and another guy.
And didn't really have hot water or anything.
And by 9:00 there was inspection, and it had to be clean.
The German came.
Well by 9:00, we would clean it up.
And then for the rest of the day, there really wasn't a hell of a lot to do.
And then we could go to the kitchen and hang around there with the kitchen help and maybe steal some bread and the whole thing.
It was very nice.
In fact, I didn't even go to sleep in the barracks.
I would sleep in, in the attic of the latrine, which was comfortable and warm and nobody bothered me.
So that lasted for a while.
Then somebody with more protekzia-- more pull than I-- got that job.
And I got a job in [GERMAN], which was a warehouse where they kept the clothing and shoes and whatnot.
And I worked there for a German kapo who was the [GERMAN] kapo, and a nice old man.
And I thought he was ancient.
Actually, he was probably considerably younger than what I am now.
And he was a green triangle, a criminal.
And of course, the kapos, most of them were green triangles, criminals.
Some of them were politicals, the red triangles, and some of them were black, conscientious objectors, your Jehovah's witnesses, priests, and so on.
Well, what happened is that we got a transport of women in.
They built a barbed wire enclosure inside the camp.
And this is where they put all the women.
And there were Hungarian women, Jewish, also some Lithuanian women, not very many.
There were maybe 300 of them.
And they brought them in to do some work, like cleaning up in the SS barracks and helping in the kitchen and so on.
So, naturally, my kapo apparently was not as ancient as I thought because he immediately got himself a woman to help him in the [GERMAN].
And I naturally got fired out of there and had to go and work at the construction site.
But, you know, by that point, I was really too young to realize what the hell was going.
I was just more puzzled than anything else.
Now I am understanding.
How many hours did you work in there?
We worked 12 hour shifts, two 12 hour shifts.
Work went on 24 hours a day, seven days a week, never a holiday, never a time off.
This is why, you know, I'm a little bit amused by the people who claim that they are due restitutions because their great-great-grandparents were slaves.
American slaves, or Brazilian slaves, or even Greek and Roman slaves-- any slave owning society, you don't pick up a slave off of the street.
You have to buy a slave.
And it's not cheap.
And a healthy, black male slave in the South, in today's money, is about $50,000, maybe $70,000.
So you don't play games with the slave.
You don't let him die.
You have to buy another one.
There is no insurance.
No more than you would kill a cow or no more than you would drive a tractor over a cliff.
Now, slavery is a terrible, terrible thing to do to a human being, you know, to export people and use them like cattle from Africa or wherever.
On the other hand, they had some value.
There was some effort to keep them alive, obviously.
In our case, there was no effort to keep us alive.
We were totally worthless.
In fact, officially, a dead Jew was a better thing than a live Jew.
And they had unlimited labor supply.
They had what they called a hospital barrack in our camp, which was a barracks, not underground, but just sort of a structure with bunks in it.
And what happened was if you couldn't walk, or if you couldn't go to work, they would put you there in a bunk.
They didn't feed you.
They didn't give you water.
They didn't give you a medicine.
And every day at 12:00 or so, the people would come in with a cart and take all the dead bodies out and dump them outside the camp and put lye on them.
And if you got sick, you died.
We had no value.
So you know, we worked.
They would get us up like at 5:00 in the morning, I think, for the day shift.
They would take us to the appellplatz, and they would count us.
And then they would count us.
And the numbers never came out because people died during the night.
So then they would look through the camp for the dead bodies.
And they'd look in the latrines.
And they'd look in the barracks.
Eventually, eventually, an hour, an hour and a half, depending, the numbers would check out.
Then we'd line up at the kitchen for the daily ration.
And the first thing they did with give us a big cup of brown water, which was warm and very nice.
And then they gave us our daily ration.
The daily ration was-- they would take a loaf of bread, which was one kilo, 2.2 pounds.
And they would divide us-- divide it.
When we first came to the camp, it was one loaf of bread for four people.
Then it was a loaf of bread for six people.
Then it was a loaf of bread for eight people towards the end of the war.
So you'd just get a slice of bread is all that was.
And then they'd give us a thing, a portion of margarine, or a slice of some kind of meat, or slice of some kind of cheese.
And most people would just eat it right away because they were too damn hungry to do anything.
Then we'd march for about an hour or so to work.
Then we'd work for six hours.
There'd be a half an hour break.
They would give us soup from a sort of a pot that they would carry to work.
And if you knew whoever it was dishing out the soup, they'd give it from the bottom and you'd get a potato or barley or something.
And if you didn't know them, they'd give from the top.
And you'd get water.
And then we'd work for another six hours and march back to camp.
They would count us again.
They'd give us another bowl of soup.
And then the rest of the night, you could do what you wanted.
But you count up the hours, there isn't much sleep left.
And with the diet we had, people were dying.
I got beaten up a couple of times.
You know, nothing really, really serious to the point where I couldn't walk.
There was punishment in the camp.
They would whip us.
And, you know, depending on what you did, you would get five [GERMAN] or 10 [GERMAN] or 15, or whatever. 20, 25, you'd probably die, because--
Yeah.
No.
Well, when I got beaten up?
No, I was never whipped.
You know, I just got hit in the face or over the back by a kapo and all that.

But you know, when I didn't move fast enough, or if I just was a little bit late taking off my hat before-- any time you saw a German, you were supposed to snap to attention one meter away, take off your hat, and say, [GERMAN].
Prisoner 82191, at your order, sir.
It was awful.
It was awful.
My father was powerless.
My father, who was the most powerful figure in my world, couldn't do anything.
He was pitiful.
And he was just as angry as I.
And I still remember what a shock it was to me-- my father smoked before the war.
And he gave it up on doctor's orders before we went into the ghetto.
And at one point towards the very end of the war, maybe a month before the end of the war, we got Red Cross packages, which was totally unexpected.
I can't-- I don't know how it happened.
And they, they cheated even on that.
They brought in the packages.
And they gave one pack-- one package for two people to be divided.
But it was like a gift from heaven.
The packages had a can of sardines.
And it had a package, a packet, maybe half a powder or a quarter pound of cubed sugar, and it had two packs of cigarettes.
And I don't remember what else.
I think that's all it had.
And people divided.
And the sugar was just great.
Everybody was sucking on the sugar.
My father exchanged his sugar for cigarettes.
He had taken up smoking again.
And he really had to.
And it was such a shock to me.
I couldn't imagine him doing such a stupid thing, and I brought him my sugar, you know, and I asked him to take it.
And he wouldn't take it.
He took a couple of cubes and sucked on them, you know, and gave me the rest.
So you know, there was that.
I might-- however, also, I want to mention German efficiency, which is really something to be admired.
You see, in the last six, seven months of the war, American airplanes started flying by, bombing Munich.
And they would come during the day, the B-29s, up in the sky, you know, lots of them.
No interference from anti-aircraft or from German aircraft.
But they would show up at the same time.
And they would pass by our worksite.
And the sirens would blow and work would stop and everybody would go in the ditch and take a little nap.
And then the sirens would-- we'd go back to work.
And then they would come back again, the sirens would blow again, because Munich was only like five, 10 minutes away by plane.
So the Germans figured it out.
They started coordinating our lunch break with the Americans.
So the sirens would blow.
And this is when they would stop work and give us our soup.
So that way you didn't have to lose a half an hour's work.
You've got to hand it to them.
They're smart bastards.
When I say that, you know, they weren't killing us, which they really weren't, other than the beatings that would kill you eventually, they killed us when they felt there was a reason for it.

And the one that I do remember was when three guys whose boots had worn out and it was winter time, and they took a blanket from a dead man and cut it into strips and wrapped it around their feet and to go to work.
And the Germans called that sabotage, you know, destroying German property, so they hanged them.
And the rest of us have to stand there for an extra hour and watch them being hanged, and then watch them dangle in the wind.
You know, it was very cold and windy that day.
And you know, so just don't fool around with German property.
So there was that.

And the work went on and then a very-- again, a very interesting, a very unusual thing happened, which again, sort of makes you wonder about human nature, or does God have a sense of humor, or whatever.
I don't know.

There was a man in the camp who, in the ghetto, had risen to a rather high position within the hierarchy because the main thing that we were doing in the ghetto in Lithuania was building an airfield for the German Air Force.
And all of us-- the 10,000 or so-- were working with that particular project.

And this man, whose name was Phillip Berkman-- and who before the war, was a salesman of-- advertising salesman for a small German language newspaper in Lithuania-- rose to be the leader of the brigade, and eventually, the leader of the whole project, or part of the project.
I was too young to go to work in the ghetto, so I don't know.
But I know that he was a big macher in the construction.
When we got to the concentration camp, Berkman also almost immediately became leader of a brigade.
He spoke perfect German.

And apparently, he knew enough about how this whole thing worked to the point where eventually he rose to be the head, from the prisoners' side, of a whole shift construction project, and eventually, the head of the entire construction project from the prisoners' side, to the point where he even had a German car and driver that drove him around, around the project, and he coordinated the prisoners, who worked when and what, and what's priority and what isn't, and straighten up screw-ups and all that.
Extremely dangerous-- you know, the first screw up, they'd shoot them.
That would be that.

And yet he managed to gain their confidence to the point where he managed to convince them that it is not efficient to have two 12 hour shifts, that it would be much more efficient to have three eight-hour shifts.
And I don't know why.
You know, labor was not a problem, unless maybe it was becoming a problem.
But they had plenty of labor.
What do they care if people died?
Berkman managed to convince them.
As far as I know, it has never happened anywhere in any other labor camp where they changed people to eight hour shifts.
And the expected happened.
People stopped dying.
You know, he must have saved hundreds and hundreds of lives.
It happened about three, maybe four months before the end of the war where we shifted to three eight hour shifts.
It was much more complicated for the Germans, I'm sure.
And yet, it worked.
So you would think that a person like that, who achieved this under circumstances like that, would probably become president of General Motors when he got out of the thing.
[NON-ENGLISH], didn't happen.
He lived in the San Fernando Valley.
He had a tiny little upholstery business.
He was a customer of ours.
We were selling him drapery fabrics.
He was a terrible nudnik.
Our people in our office didn't even like talking to him, because he was always complaining about things.
And he died about 10 years ago of natural causes.
A nebbish, a nothing in normal life-- What was his name?
His name was Phillip Berkman, who achieved greatness and saved many lives under conditions that were not normal and could not function in normal life.
How long?
Slightly over a year.
I have no idea.
I have no idea.
I remember him hitting me in the camp.
Afterwards you know, in peacetime, I never held it against him.
You know, I was not getting into line fast enough when they were counting, or whatever the hell it is.
But he-- you know, we had a good friendly relationship.
He used to come over to my parents' house, to my mother's house and play poker with her.
I was liberated at the camp.
Again, I did the usual.
I didn't follow orders.
When the Allies were coming closer-- my father died on April 7th, 20 days before liberation.
He just died.
He, he was becoming a muselmann from exhaustion and from-- a muselmann is the slang term for being-- for being a walking dead-- the skeleton-like matchstick arms and legs.
And they put him in the hospital barrack the morning when he couldn't go to work.
And when I came back from work that night, I went to see him.
It was all right.
They let you go in if you wanted to.
And I sat on the side of his bed, and he was hardly breathing.
And then he died.
Yes.
Well, there were lots of dead bodies lying around all over the place.
It wasn't anything unusual.
I remember walking out, and you know, I was screaming.
I remember that.
And I felt very frightened.
And a man came up to me, another prisoner.
He said, what are you screaming for, boy?
And I said, my father just died.
And he sort of patted me on the head, and said-- and he said that's it.
Didn't say anything.
Just walked away.
And that was that.
Yes.
Yes, of course.
There was nothing else I could do.
Well, when the front started approaching, we could almost hear the artillery.
And the Germans decided to evacuate all 10 camps.
And they started bringing in the people from other camps into our camp, which was number one.
And they would form a transport and march them out, and form another transport and march them out.
And I decided I would stay as long as I could.
I had stolen a half a loaf of bread from the kitchen about a week before.
It was getting dried up, but it was still good.
And I sort of decided that if they march us out, I will try and escape as soon as I can and hide in the forest and wait for the army to come in.
Because I knew the Allies were coming because from time to time, we would clean up around the SS barracks, or would see an old newspaper, or we'd hear the radio a little bit.
So we knew what was happening.
We knew when Roosevelt died, for example.
We knew that there was an invasion of Normandy.
We knew the Allies are going across Europe and coming closer.
Well, I hid in the barracks under the straw.
And I was just lying there.
And then after a while, it was very quiet.
I couldn't hear anything.
So I came out to look around, and the camp was empty.
There were some dead people lying around, but I couldn't see any Germans.
I did the logical thing.
I went to the kitchen.
And in the kitchen, I found 12, maybe 15 people who were also left behind.
And there was no food.
So we spent the night sort of huddling together, not knowing what-- the electricity was shut off.
Didn't work.
The next morning, the lights-- you know, sun came up, and somebody said, they must have mined the camp.
It'll probably blow up any minute.
We better get out of here.
And it sounded logical.
So we walked out of the kitchen.
We walked to the gate.
We opened the gate, and we're walking outside the camp.
There was a big, green field on one side.
We were walking along, and all of a sudden, we heard shooting and bullets were whistling over our head, so we all dropped on the ground.
And I remember thinking, ah ha, the Germans are back, you know?
They're going to kill us off now.
And then we saw tanks coming towards us on the horizon across the field.
And then we saw one guy jump up and down, and yelling and screaming and waving his arms.
And he was pointing at the tanks, and the tanks had white stars on them.
So it was the Americans.
It was-- and then all of a sudden, there were trucks on the road, the American trucks.
And the Americans were throwing food down from the trucks-- white bread which didn't taste like any white bread I ever had.
It wasn't good.
But it was delicious.
And they were giving us little c-rations, little round cans with spam and ham and cheese.
And they were throwing cigarettes.

And then the next thing you knew, a couple of trucks pulled up, and soldiers came out with big sort of pumps with blunt noses, and they pumped DDT into our sleeves and pants and everything to kill the lice.
And then I saw a couple of Hungarians who had gotten a horse and a wagon, and they were on the horse and a wagon spurring it on, yelling, we're going to Budapest.
We're going to Budapest.
I don't know how far they got, but that's where they were going.
And it was a sort of a celebratory thing.
There were some people got together in a circle were beating up a kapo.
The Germans had already been go-- had already gone.
And then all of us were taken to sort of a hospital complex in Landsberg, which became the displaced person camp for that particular bunch of people.

And little by little, others started coming from around the countryside from other camps to the point where that hospital complex had, oh, maybe 1,000, 1,500 people in it-- mostly men-- just a few women, and hardly any kids.
I immediately started working in that hospital, primarily because they had an ambulance that the Soviet POW who was the ambulance driver would let me drive.
So I enjoyed that very much.
And mostly, I didn't do anything.
I just lay around, you know, waiting to see what's going to happen next and reading books.
Oh, that was much later.
That was much later.
At first, I didn't really know what to do.
I knew my father was dead.
I didn't know if my mother was alive or not.
So I decided I might as well go back home to Lithuania because I wanted to go to school.
I was 14 and a half when I was liberated.
I knew I was ignorant.
I didn't go to school in a while.
And everybody was coming by from the different countries taking their citizens back to wherever they belonged.
And the Soviets wanted their citizens back.
Much later, after I came to the States, I found out the Soviets not only were looking for them, they were demanding that they be turned over.

And they blackmailed Stalin-- they blackmailed Churchill into making sure that all Soviet citizens are returned by force to be executed or imprisoned, or else Stalin wasn't going to give back the British POWs to the British.
But I didn't know that.
I voluntarily went and registered to go back.
And by the time my transport was ready to go, I was waiting for it in Munich, and some people from the previous transport started running back to Germany.
And one guy came to us, and he said, hey, don't go because everybody gets put in a camp, and everybody goes before a commission.
Everybody gets asked the same question.
Why did Hitler let you survive if he wanted to kill all Jews?
Well, if he let you live, then you're a collaborator because you must have done something for them.
And then bad things happened.
So I decided not to go back.
And I hitched a ride back to my displaced person camp in Landsberg.
And I came back, and oh my god, everybody was really very nervous.
They were not happy to see me at all because they thought the Russians would come looking for me, my name was on the list, and that it would be difficult for people.
It was getting to be very unpleasant.

And then just as if by coincidence, all of a sudden, a whole bunch of military-- British military-- this was an American zone-- but British military trucks with a steering wheel on the wrong side rolled into the DP camp.
And all these British military trucks have a big star of David on the side.
And I thought, what is that?
And there is British soldiers, and it says Palestine with the Star of David.
And it was the British-- the Palestinian Brigade of the British army, the 30,000 volunteers that the British finally permitted to volunteer to fight with the Allies.
There was, of course, also a division of the Arabs and the Muslims, but that was not with the British Army.
There was the SS Muslim division with the mufti sitting in Berlin.

So there was also the-- speaking of the Bosnian situation, there was the Bosnia Herzegovnia SS battalion who were so terrible in the Ukraine that the Germans had to pull them out because of the atrocities they committed against the peaceful population.
But that's another story.
Anyway, the British-- the Palestinian brigade was stationed in Italy.
And they would ostensibly bring supplies to the British army in Germany and supposedly would go back empty.
In fact, they carried Jews back to Italy.
And every time the transports went, they carried a couple thousand Jews back to Italy.
The most amazing thing was that one of the drivers in this particular column was a cousin of mine.
